fields.name: Barbell Back Squat
fields.description: The king of lower body exercises. Bar rests on upper traps, feet shoulder-width apart with toes slightly flared. Descend until thighs are parallel or below, keeping a neutral spine throughout. Drive through the heels to return to standing, keeping knees tracking over toes. Brace the core hard before each rep and maintain upper back tightness to avoid good-morning squats.

fields.name: Conventional Deadlift
fields.muscle_group: Hamstrings, Glutes, Lower Back, Traps, Forearms
fields.description: The ultimate posterior chain developer. Bar over mid-foot, grip just outside the legs. Hinge at the hips, flatten the back, and drive the floor away rather than pulling the bar up. Keep the bar dragging against the shins on the way up. Lock out hips and knees simultaneously at the top without hyperextending the lower back. Lower with control by hinging at the hips first.

fields.name: Flat Barbell Bench Press
fields.description: Foundational horizontal pressing movement. Lie with shoulder blades retracted and depressed, slight arch in lower back, feet flat on the floor. Grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. Lower the bar to the lower chest under control, then press back up in a slight arc toward the upper chest. Avoid flaring the elbows excessively — keep them at 60-75 degrees from the torso for shoulder safety.

fields.name: Overhead Press (Barbell)
fields.description: Standing barbell press for total shoulder development. Start with the bar at clavicle height, elbows slightly forward. Press the bar straight overhead, moving your head back slightly to allow clearance, then re-position head once the bar passes the forehead. Lock out fully at the top with ears between arms. Brace the abs and glutes throughout to prevent lower back hyperextension.

fields.name: Bent-Over Barbell Row
fields.muscle_group: Latissimus Dorsi, Rhomboids, Rear Deltoids, Biceps
fields.description: Essential compound pulling movement for upper back thickness. Hinge at the hips until the torso is approximately 45 degrees or parallel to the floor. Pull the bar to the lower chest or upper abdomen, driving elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades together at the top. Keep the lower back neutral — do not round. Control the eccentric descent for maximum muscle engagement.

fields.name: Romanian Deadlift
fields.description: Excellent hip hinge movement for posterior chain development with emphasis on hamstring lengthening. Start standing with the bar at hip height. Push the hips back while lowering the bar along the legs, feeling a strong stretch in the hamstrings. Stop when the back begins to round (usually mid-shin level). Drive the hips forward to return to standing. Keep a soft bend in the knees throughout.

fields.name: Incline Barbell Bench Press
fields.description: Targets the clavicular head of the pectoral muscle more effectively than flat pressing. Set bench to 30-45 degrees. Grip slightly narrower than flat bench. Lower the bar to the upper chest and press back up, maintaining the same shoulder blade position as flat bench. Avoid excessive weight — the incline angle significantly reduces the load you can handle compared to flat pressing.

fields.name: Sumo Deadlift
fields.description: Wide-stance deadlift variation that reduces the range of motion and places greater emphasis on the hip musculature and quads. Feet 1.5-2x shoulder-width, toes pointed out 45 degrees. Push knees out hard throughout the lift. The torso angle is more upright than conventional, making it favorable for those with longer torsos or hip anatomy suited to the stance.

fields.name: Front Squat
fields.description: Bar is held in the front rack position (fingertips only) or crossed-arm position on the front deltoids. Requires significant thoracic spine mobility and core stability. The upright torso position shifts emphasis to the quadriceps compared to the back squat. Keep elbows high throughout the descent and ascent. Excellent for developing quad strength and improving squat mechanics.

fields.name: Close-Grip Bench Press
fields.description: Bench press variation with hands shoulder-width apart or slightly narrower to maximize tricep recruitment. The reduced grip width shifts stress away from the pectorals onto the triceps. Keep elbows tucked close to the body throughout. Do not go too narrow as this can cause wrist discomfort. Excellent for building tricep mass and improving lockout strength for competition bench press.

fields.name: Barbell Hip Thrust
fields.description: The most effective exercise for direct glute loading. Upper back on bench, bar across hips on a pad. Drive through heels and squeeze glutes hard to extend the hips until body forms a straight line from shoulders to knees. Hold for 1-2 seconds at the top. Keep chin tucked to maintain neutral neck. The range of motion is relatively short but the glute activation is extremely high throughout.

fields.name: Pendlay Row
fields.muscle_group: Latissimus Dorsi, Rhomboids, Rear Deltoids, Biceps
fields.description: Strict barbell row where the bar returns to the floor between each rep, allowing a powerful concentric pull from a dead stop. Torso nearly parallel to the floor. This eliminates momentum and ensures each rep is executed with full effort. The dead stop also allows heavier loading than a traditional bent-over row. Excellent for developing explosive pulling strength.

fields.name: Deficit Deadlift
fields.description: Performed while standing on a 2-4 inch platform, increasing the range of motion compared to a standard deadlift. This forces greater hip and knee flexion off the floor, developing leg drive and strength in the weakest position. Excellent for improving pull from the floor in powerlifting and for building overall posterior chain strength. Use lighter weights than regular deadlift.

fields.name: Pause Squat
fields.description: Squat variation with a 2-3 second pause at the bottom of the movement. Eliminates the stretch-shortening reflex, forcing pure muscular effort to initiate the ascent. Dramatically improves positional strength and exposes weaknesses in squat mechanics. Keep tension throughout the pause — do not relax. Use 70-80% of your regular squat weight. Excellent competition prep exercise.

fields.name: Good Morning
fields.description: Bar on the back (like a squat), hinge at the hips with a slight knee bend until torso is nearly parallel to the floor, then return to standing. Excellent for developing spinal erector and hamstring strength, and teaches the hip hinge pattern under load. Keep a neutral spine — do not round the lower back. Often used as an accessory for deadlift and squat improvement.

fields.name: Push Press
fields.description: Barbell overhead press that uses a leg drive dip to generate momentum, allowing significantly more weight to be pressed overhead than a strict press. Dip slightly by bending knees 3-4 inches, then explosively drive upward and transfer momentum into pressing the bar overhead. Excellent for developing overhead strength and power. Strict press the weight down under control.

fields.name: Barbell Walking Lunges
fields.description: Barbell across the upper back, step forward into a lunge, lower the back knee toward the floor, then drive through the front heel to step the back foot forward into the next lunge. Develops unilateral leg strength, balance, and coordination. Keep the torso vertical throughout. Excellent for addressing strength imbalances between legs and developing functional athletic strength.

fields.name: Zercher Squat
fields.muscle_group: Quadriceps, Biceps, Core, Upper Back, Glutes
fields.description: Unusual but highly effective squat variation where the barbell is held in the crook of the elbows with arms crossed in front. Forces an extremely upright torso and requires significant core and upper back engagement to keep the bar secure. Develops tremendous core strength and quad dominance. Can be hard on the arms — use padding if needed. Excellent cross-training movement.

fields.name: Box Squat
fields.description: Squat to a box or bench set just below parallel, sitting back onto the surface briefly before driving up. Teaches proper sit-back squat mechanics and develops strength specifically in the hole. The brief pause on the box eliminates momentum. Excellent for powerlifters and those learning to properly load the posterior chain in the squat. Use slightly wider stance than regular squat.

fields.name: Rack Pull
fields.muscle_group: Upper Back, Traps, Hamstrings, Glutes, Forearms
fields.description: Deadlift performed from pins set at knee height or above, reducing the range of motion. Allows heavier loads than a full deadlift, overloading the upper back, traps, and grip. Excellent for breaking through deadlift plateaus by building strength in the lockout portion. Also useful for those with poor hip mobility who cannot safely pull from the floor.

fields.name: Dumbbell Bicep Curl
fields.description: Fundamental bicep isolation exercise. Stand or sit with dumbbells at sides, supinate the wrist as you curl to maximize bicep activation. Avoid swinging the torso — keep elbows pinned at the sides. Fully extend at the bottom and squeeze hard at the top. Can be performed alternating or simultaneously. Try both pronated (neutral) and supinated grips for complete bicep development.

fields.name: Cable Tricep Pushdown
fields.description: Isolation exercise for the triceps using a cable machine with a rope or bar attachment. Keep elbows pinned at the sides throughout. Extend fully at the bottom and control the return. The cable provides constant tension throughout the range of motion, unlike free weights. Rope attachment allows the wrists to rotate at the bottom for slightly greater range of motion and outer head activation.

fields.name: Lateral Raise
fields.description: Isolation movement for the side delts to create shoulder width. Raise dumbbells to the side until arms are parallel to the floor. Keep a slight bend in the elbows and lead with the elbows rather than the hands. Avoid shrugging at the top. Use relatively light weight — the lateral delt is a small muscle. Performing with cables provides more constant tension and better results at low weights.

fields.name: Face Pull
fields.muscle_group: Rear Deltoids, Rotator Cuff, Rhomboids, Traps
fields.description: Crucial exercise for shoulder health and posterior deltoid development. Set cable at face height with rope attachment. Pull toward the face with elbows high, externally rotating the shoulders at the peak. Think of pulling the rope apart at the end position. High reps work best (15-25). An essential exercise for anyone who does heavy pressing, counteracting internal rotation and protecting the shoulder joint.

fields.name: Cable Fly
fields.description: Isolation movement for the chest using cables set at shoulder height or above. Step forward with one foot, lean slightly forward, and bring the handles together in a hugging motion in front of the chest. Keep a slight bend in the elbows. The cable maintains tension at the fully contracted position unlike dumbbells. Excellent finishing exercise for chest after compound pressing movements.

fields.name: Hammer Curl
fields.description: Neutral-grip curl that targets the brachialis and brachioradialis more than standard curls, adding thickness to the arm and forearm. Hold dumbbells with a neutral grip (thumbs up) throughout the movement. Can be performed alternating, simultaneously, or across the body (cross-body hammer curl). Excellent for overall arm development when combined with standard curls.

fields.name: Overhead Tricep Extension
fields.description: Targets the long head of the tricep, which is best activated with the arm elevated above the head. Can be performed with a dumbbell, EZ bar, or cable. Keep elbows close and pointing forward throughout. Lower slowly behind the head and extend fully. The long head crosses the shoulder joint, so overhead position fully stretches it. Use moderate weight and focus on the stretch and squeeze.

fields.name: Single-Arm Dumbbell Row
fields.muscle_group: Latissimus Dorsi, Rhomboids, Rear Deltoids, Biceps
fields.description: Unilateral back exercise allowing a full range of motion and heavy loading. Support one hand and same-side knee on a bench. Pull the dumbbell from a full hang position to the hip, driving the elbow past the torso. Rotate the torso slightly at the top to increase range of motion. Full stretch at the bottom maximizes lat engagement. Heavier than most bilateral row variations due to stable support.

fields.name: Cable Crunch
fields.description: Weighted abdominal exercise using a cable machine with rope attachment. Kneel facing the machine, rope behind the neck. Crunch down by rounding the spine, bringing the elbows toward the knees. The movement is spinal flexion — not a hip hinge. Pause at the bottom and squeeze the abs. The cable provides constant tension unlike crunches on the floor. Allows progressive overload of the abdominal muscles.

fields.name: Cable Row (Seated)
fields.description: Seated cable row with a close-grip or wide-grip attachment. Sit tall, feet on the platform, slight bend in the knees. Pull the handle to the lower abdomen, driving elbows back and squeezing shoulder blades together. Return with a full stretch — allow the shoulder blades to protract at the end of each rep for full range of motion. Do not lean back excessively to generate momentum.

fields.name: Preacher Curl
fields.description: Curl performed on a preacher bench that locks the elbows in place, preventing cheating and isolating the bicep. Use an EZ bar or dumbbells. Lower fully to a complete extension at the bottom (carefully, to avoid bicep tendon strain) and curl to peak contraction. The preacher bench removes the ability to swing, making this a strict isolation exercise. Excellent for building bicep peak.

fields.name: Dumbbell Shoulder Press
fields.description: Seated or standing dumbbell press for shoulder development. Greater range of motion than barbell pressing as dumbbells can be lowered further. Allows independent arm movement, which can help identify and correct imbalances. Press the dumbbells overhead until arms are fully extended, then lower to ear level. Avoid arching the lower back excessively when seated. Excellent compound shoulder movement.

fields.name: Incline Dumbbell Curl
fields.description: Performed lying back on an incline bench with arms hanging freely. The stretched starting position places maximum tension on the long head of the bicep. Curl both arms simultaneously without swinging. The incline position prevents any compensation and provides a deep stretch at the bottom. One of the best exercises for developing bicep peak. Use lighter weight than standing curls.

fields.name: Reverse Fly
fields.description: Isolation exercise for the posterior shoulder and upper back. Can be performed with dumbbells (bent over), on a pec deck machine reversed, or with cables. Raise the arms to the side in a wide arc with a slight bend in the elbows. Squeeze the rear delts at the top. Crucial for balanced shoulder development and posture correction, especially important for those who do a lot of pressing work.

fields.name: Dumbbell Romanian Deadlift
fields.description: Same hip hinge movement as the barbell version but with dumbbells, which can allow the weights to travel closer to the body on each side. The dumbbells can be positioned outside the legs, which may be more comfortable for some people. Excellent for developing hamstring flexibility and strength. Single-leg variation is highly effective for addressing imbalances and improving balance.

fields.name: Goblet Squat
fields.description: Squat holding a dumbbell or kettlebell vertically at the chest. The front-loaded position encourages an upright torso, making it an excellent teaching tool for squat mechanics. Elbows point down inside the knees at the bottom. Excellent for improving squat depth and mobility. Beginners and advanced athletes alike benefit from this movement — add heavy weight for a serious quad and glute workout.

fields.name: Dumbbell Step-Up
fields.description: Step onto a box or bench holding dumbbells at the sides. Drive through the heel of the elevated foot to lift the body up, bringing the trailing foot to stand on the box. Lower with control. Keep the torso upright and avoid pushing off the ground foot. Excellent unilateral lower body exercise that develops balance, coordination, and single-leg strength. Height of the box determines the range of motion.

fields.name: Arnold Press
fields.description: Dumbbell shoulder press variation developed by Arnold Schwarzenegger. Start with dumbbells in front of the face, palms facing you, then rotate the palms outward as you press overhead. Reverse the rotation on the way down. The rotating motion engages more of the deltoid than a standard press. Keep the movement fluid and controlled. Excellent for complete anterior and lateral deltoid development.

fields.name: Zottman Curl
fields.description: Unique curl that combines a supinated curl upward with a pronated lowering, training both the bicep (supinated) and brachioradialis (pronated) in one movement. Curl the weight up with palms facing up, rotate to palms-down at the top, then lower slowly. The slow pronated descent is extremely challenging on the forearm extensors. One of the most complete arm exercises available.

fields.name: Chest-Supported Row
fields.description: Dumbbell row performed lying face-down on an incline bench. The chest support eliminates lower back involvement and any cheating with body lean, making it a pure upper back isolation exercise. Row the dumbbells toward the hips, driving elbows back and squeezing shoulder blades. Excellent for people with lower back issues who cannot perform bent-over rows safely.

fields.name: Leg Press
fields.description: Machine-based leg exercise performed lying at an angle or seated. Place feet shoulder-width apart, mid-platform. Press the platform away and lower under control until knees are at 90 degrees or deeper. Do not allow the lower back to lift off the seat at the bottom. High foot placement emphasizes glutes and hamstrings; low placement targets quads. Allows very heavy loading with spinal support.

fields.name: Leg Extension
fields.description: Machine isolation exercise that targets the quadriceps through knee extension. Sit with the back of the knees at the edge of the pad and extend the legs to full lockout. Squeeze the quads at the top. Lower slowly and do not swing. Recent research suggests leg extensions are safe for the knees when performed through full range of motion. Best used as an accessory after heavy compound leg work.

fields.name: Seated Hamstring Curl
fields.description: Machine exercise for isolating the hamstrings. The seated position keeps the hip flexed, which fully stretches the hamstrings and allows greater range of motion than lying curls. Curl the pad down, hold briefly at full contraction, and return slowly. Keep hips pressed against the seat. Excellent for hamstring hypertrophy and a great complement to hip hinge movements like RDL.

fields.name: Standing Calf Raise
fields.description: Can be performed on a dedicated machine, with a barbell, or with dumbbells. Rise onto the balls of the feet to full extension, hold at the top for 1-2 seconds, and lower with a full stretch. A stretched starting position (heels below the platform) provides a greater range of motion. The gastrocnemius responds better to heavier loads with lower reps; the soleus with higher reps and a bent knee position.

fields.name: Hip Abduction Machine
fields.description: Seated machine that targets the outer glutes by pushing the knees apart against resistance. Essential for developing glute medius strength, which is crucial for knee stability, hip stability, and preventing knee valgus in squats and daily movement. Keep the movement controlled and feel the squeeze in the outer glute at the peak. Often overlooked by men but critical for overall glute development and injury prevention.

fields.name: Lat Pulldown
fields.description: Cable machine exercise that mimics the pull-up. Sit with thighs under the pad, grip slightly wider than shoulder-width. Pull the bar to the upper chest, driving elbows down and back. Lean back slightly (10-15 degrees). Return with full arm extension to fully stretch the lats. Avoid using momentum or pulling the bar behind the neck. The lat pulldown is excellent for building the width of the back.

fields.name: Chest Press Machine
fields.description: Machine version of the bench press, useful for beginners learning pressing mechanics, those with shoulder issues, or as a finishing exercise. Allows independent arm movement to identify imbalances. The fixed path reduces stabilizer demand, allowing focus on the target muscles. Adjust the seat so the handles align with the mid-chest. Excellent for high-rep hypertrophy work and drop sets.

fields.name: Shoulder Press Machine
fields.description: Machine overhead press with fixed movement path. Adjust the seat so that handles start at shoulder height. Press overhead to full extension, keeping the core engaged and avoiding excessive lower back arch. Safer for those with rotator cuff issues compared to heavy barbell pressing. Useful for training to failure safely since the machine guides the path and the weight cannot fall.

fields.name: Seated Cable Row Machine
fields.description: Machine-guided rowing movement with a fixed cable angle. Sit tall, pull the handles to the abdomen, driving elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades. The machine path removes lower back stabilization demands compared to free-weight rows. Excellent for learning proper rowing mechanics or as a high-volume back accessory. Change grip attachments to vary the emphasis.

fields.name: Assisted Pull-Up Machine
fields.description: Machine that counterbalances your bodyweight to allow you to perform pull-ups with assistance. The more weight selected, the lighter the effective load. Allows beginners to build toward unassisted pull-ups by progressively reducing the assistance. Keep the same technique as a regular pull-up: drive elbows down, chest to the bar, full extension at the bottom.

fields.name: Pull-Up
fields.description: One of the best upper body exercises. Hang from a bar with a pronated grip (palms away), slightly wider than shoulder-width. Pull yourself up until your chin clears the bar, driving elbows down toward your hips. Lower with full control to a dead hang. Variations include neutral grip (chin-up style) and supinated grip (chin-up). Add weight with a belt once bodyweight becomes too easy.

fields.name: Push-Up
fields.description: Foundational bodyweight pressing movement. Hands slightly wider than shoulder-width, body in a straight plank position from head to heels. Lower until the chest nearly touches the floor, elbows at 45 degrees. Press back up. Progressions include incline (easier), deficit (harder), archer, and one-arm push-ups. Focus on keeping the core rigid — do not allow the hips to sag or pike up.

fields.name: Parallel Bar Dip
fields.description: Compound bodyweight exercise. Support yourself on parallel bars with arms locked out. Lower by bending the elbows, allowing a slight forward lean to emphasize the chest (or staying upright for tricep focus). Descend until upper arms are parallel to the floor. Press back to lockout. Once bodyweight is easy, add a dipping belt. One of the best mass builders for the triceps and lower chest.

fields.name: Plank
fields.muscle_group: Core (transverse abdominis, rectus abdominis, obliques), Glutes, Shoulders
fields.description: Fundamental core stability exercise. Forearm plank: elbows under shoulders, body in a straight line from head to heels. Do not let hips sag or pike. Breathe normally through the hold. Progressions include straight-arm plank, RKC plank (squeeze everything), shoulder taps, and plank with leg lift. Quality of position matters more than duration — stop when form breaks down rather than fighting through poor form.

fields.name: Side Plank
fields.description: Lateral core stability exercise. Lie on your side, prop up on forearm with elbow under shoulder, stack feet or stagger them for stability. Lift hips off the floor so the body forms a straight line. Hold for time. Do not let the hip sag. Progression includes hip dips (add hip drop and lift for dynamic version) and adding a top leg raise. Critical for addressing oblique weakness and lateral stability.

fields.name: Hollow Body Hold
fields.muscle_group: Core (rectus abdominis, hip flexors, transverse abdominis)
fields.description: Gymnastics-based core exercise. Lie on the back, press lower back into the floor, extend arms overhead and legs out straight. Raise shoulders and legs off the floor to find the 'hollow' position. The body creates a banana-like concave shape. The lower back must stay pressed to the floor throughout. This is the foundation of all gymnastics core work. Regress by bending knees or keeping arms at sides if needed.

fields.name: L-Sit (Parallel Bars)
fields.description: Advanced isometric hold on parallel bars or dip bars. Support your body on straight arms and hold the legs out horizontally in an L-shape. Requires significant hip flexor strength and compression. Progress from tucked L-sit to single-leg extended to full L-sit. Hold for 5-10 seconds initially, building up time progressively. Develops tremendous core and hip flexor strength essential for gymnastics movements.

fields.name: Pistol Squat
fields.description: Advanced single-leg squat where the non-working leg is extended forward parallel to the floor. Requires excellent ankle mobility, hip mobility, quad strength, and balance. Lower all the way down until the hamstring touches the calf, then drive back up. Progressions: assisted pistol (with TRX), box pistol, partial range, then full pistol. Highly functional movement for unilateral lower body strength.

fields.name: Nordic Curl
fields.description: Highly effective hamstring exercise with strong evidence for injury prevention, especially hamstring strains in athletes. Fix feet under a pad or partner, start kneeling upright, and lower the body forward under control using the hamstrings. Catch yourself with hands at the bottom, then use minimal push-up assistance to return. The eccentric loading is extreme — beginners will only manage a few degrees before needing assistance.

fields.name: Inverted Row
fields.description: Bodyweight horizontal pulling movement, also called the Australian pull-up. Set a bar at waist height, hang underneath with arms extended, and pull the chest to the bar. The more horizontal the body, the harder the exercise. Excellent beginner progression toward pull-ups and a great scapular retraction exercise. Keep the body rigid — no hip sagging. Elevate feet to increase difficulty.

fields.name: Pike Push-Up
fields.description: Bodyweight overhead pressing exercise and progression toward handstand push-ups. Get into a downward dog position with hips high. Lower the head toward the floor between the hands, then press back up. The more vertical the body, the more it resembles an overhead press. Progressions: pike push-up, elevated pike, wall-supported handstand push-up. Excellent for shoulder strength without equipment.

fields.name: Single-Leg Glute Bridge
fields.description: Lie on back, one foot flat on the floor, other leg extended. Drive through the heel to lift the hips until the body forms a straight line from shoulder to knee. Squeeze the glute hard at the top. The single-leg version doubles the load on each glute compared to the bilateral version and adds a rotational stability challenge. Excellent rehabilitation exercise and glute activator before heavy training.

fields.name: Superman Hold
fields.description: Lie face down on the floor. Simultaneously raise both arms, chest, and legs off the floor and hold. Squeeze the glutes and lower back at the top. Develops posterior chain endurance and is an excellent prehabilitation exercise for the lower back. Great as part of a morning routine or as a warm-up activation drill. Progress with alternating arm/leg raises and eventually weighted holds.

fields.name: Bear Crawl
fields.description: Start on all fours with knees hovering 1-2 inches off the floor. Move forward by simultaneously moving the right hand and left foot, then left hand and right foot. The knees remain off the floor throughout. Challenges core anti-rotation, shoulder stability, and coordination. Can be performed forward, backward, and laterally. Excellent warm-up movement and conditioning drill when performed for distance or time.

fields.name: Wall Sit
fields.description: Stand with back flat against a wall and slide down until thighs are parallel to the floor, knees at 90 degrees. Hold for time. Can add weight by placing a plate on the thighs. An excellent quad endurance exercise requiring no equipment. Commonly used in athletic training, physiotherapy, and as a challenge between sets. The longer you hold the position, the greater the metabolic stress on the quads.

fields.name: Interval Running
fields.description: Alternating high-intensity sprint intervals with active recovery jogs. Classic protocol: 30 sec sprint / 90 sec jog x 8-10 rounds. Work periods should feel like 85-95% maximum effort. The combination of aerobic and anaerobic work creates superior cardiovascular adaptations and caloric burn compared to steady-state cardio. Warm up for 5-10 minutes before starting intervals. Cool down thoroughly afterward.

fields.name: Jump Rope
fields.description: Highly efficient cardio exercise that develops coordination, timing, and cardiovascular endurance. Basic two-foot jump: land on the balls of the feet, slight knee bend, wrists drive the rope (not arms). Progressions: single leg, alternating feet, double-unders (rope passes twice per jump). Double-unders are extremely demanding metabolically. 10 minutes of jump rope is equivalent to approximately 30 minutes of jogging in cardiovascular benefit.

fields.name: Rowing Machine Intervals
fields.muscle_group: Back, Legs, Arms, Core, Cardiovascular System
fields.description: Full body cardio using the rowing ergometer. Proper technique is crucial: legs initiate the drive (not the arms), then lean back slightly, then pull the handle to the lower ribs. Recovery phase reverses this sequence. Interval protocols: 500m repeats with equal rest, or tabata-style 20 sec on / 10 sec off. One of the few truly full-body cardio exercises that simultaneously develops strength and endurance.

fields.name: Assault Bike Sprints
fields.description: Air bike with fan resistance that increases with speed, making it impossible to 'cheat' the effort. Push and pull the handles while pedaling for full-body engagement. Sprint protocols: 10-15 second all-out bursts with 45-second rest x 10, or 30/30 intervals. The assault bike is notorious for its difficulty — caloric burn is extremely high. Excellent for building anaerobic capacity and metabolic conditioning.

fields.name: Stair Climber
fields.description: Cardio machine that simulates climbing stairs. Step fully onto each step, pushing through the heel to maximize glute engagement. Avoid leaning heavily on the handrails — this cheats the effort and reduces caloric burn. Steady-state use at moderate intensity is excellent for lower body shaping; interval use (alternating fast and slow climbing) provides superior cardiovascular conditioning. Easy on the joints compared to running.

fields.name: Box Jump
fields.muscle_group: Quadriceps, Glutes, Calves, Hip Flexors (power development)
fields.description: Plyometric exercise for developing lower body explosive power. Stand in front of a box, dip slightly into a quarter squat, then swing arms and jump onto the box, landing softly in a quarter squat position. Step down (do not jump down repeatedly — this increases injury risk). Focus on the quality of each jump, not speed. Select a box height where you can land with excellent form. Develops reactive strength and sports performance.

fields.name: Sled Push
fields.description: Load a sled with weight and push it across the floor by leaning into it at approximately 45 degrees with arms extended. Drive with powerful leg strides, keeping a straight body angle. The sled push is one of the most metabolically demanding exercises and is very kind to the joints (purely concentric muscle action). Excellent for conditioning, fat loss, and developing leg power. Start light to learn mechanics.

fields.name: Battle Ropes
fields.description: Alternating or simultaneous wave patterns with thick ropes anchored to a wall. Creates significant metabolic demand while developing shoulder endurance and grip strength. Patterns include alternating waves, double waves, slams, circles, and side-to-side. Keep the core engaged and maintain an athletic stance. Typically performed in 20-40 second bursts with rest periods. One of the best tools for building work capacity and conditioning.

fields.name: Burpee
fields.muscle_group: Full Body: Chest, Shoulders, Core, Quadriceps, Cardiovascular System
fields.description: High-intensity total body exercise. From standing: squat down, place hands on the floor, jump or step feet back to a push-up position, perform a push-up, jump feet to hands, stand and jump with hands overhead. Each rep is a complete movement sequence. Scales by removing the push-up or jump. One of the most efficient exercises for burning calories and developing overall fitness. 10 minutes of burpees burns approximately 100-150 calories.

fields.name: Mountain Climber
fields.muscle_group: Core, Hip Flexors, Shoulders, Cardiovascular System
fields.description: Start in a high plank position. Drive one knee toward the chest, then quickly switch legs in a running motion while maintaining the plank. The pace can vary from slow and controlled (core focus) to fast and continuous (cardio focus). Keep the hips level — avoid piking up or letting them sag. Excellent for combining core stability training with metabolic conditioning. Can be performed on sliders for increased difficulty.

fields.name: Kettlebell Swing
fields.muscle_group: Glutes, Hamstrings, Lower Back, Core, Shoulders
fields.description: Ballistic hip hinge movement using a kettlebell. Hike the kettlebell back between the legs, then explosively drive the hips forward to propel the bell to chest or overhead height. The power comes from the hips, not the arms or shoulders. Stand tall at the top with glutes squeezed hard. Russian swing stops at chest height; American swing goes overhead. Master the deadlift hip hinge pattern before loading the swing with weight.

fields.name: Turkish Get-Up
fields.muscle_group: Full Body: Shoulder Stability, Core, Hip Flexors, Glutes
fields.description: Complex multi-step movement performed with a kettlebell held overhead throughout. From lying with one arm extended overhead to standing and back down, the exercise requires total body coordination, shoulder stability, and mobility. 6 steps in each direction. Extremely effective for identifying and correcting movement asymmetries and building functional strength. Start without weight, then with a shoe balanced on the fist before adding a kettlebell.

fields.name: Kettlebell Clean & Press
fields.description: Two-part movement: clean the kettlebell from the floor to the rack position (fist at shoulder, bell resting on forearm), then press it overhead to lockout. Requires coordinated hip drive and upper body strength. The clean is a pulling-with-the-hips movement, not an arm curl. Develops total body coordination and pressing strength. Can be performed as a single clean-and-press or alternating between cleans and presses.

fields.name: Farmer's Walk
fields.muscle_group: Forearms, Traps, Core, Legs, Cardiovascular System
fields.description: Pick up heavy dumbbells or kettlebells and walk for distance or time. Simple but brutally effective for developing grip strength, trap development, core stability, and overall work capacity. Keep shoulders pulled back and down, chest tall. Walk with purpose — not shuffling. Loaded carries are one of the most underutilized exercises in most training programs. Excellent finisher for any workout or standalone conditioning tool.

fields.name: Thruster
fields.description: Combination of a front squat and overhead press performed in one continuous movement. Hold dumbbells or a barbell at shoulder height, squat to full depth, then use the upward momentum from the squat to drive the weight overhead. A deeply demanding exercise that taxes the entire body and the cardiovascular system simultaneously. A staple of CrossFit programming. Use a weight that allows full overhead lockout.

fields.name: Wall Ball Shot
fields.description: Hold a medicine ball at chest height, squat to full depth, then use the explosive upward drive to propel the ball upward to a target on the wall (typically 10 feet for men, 9 feet for women). Catch the ball as it returns and use it to descend directly into the next squat. Continuous rhythmic movement. Develops power, cardiovascular endurance, and total body coordination. Popular in CrossFit workouts.

fields.name: Power Clean
fields.muscle_group: Quadriceps, Glutes, Hamstrings, Traps, Shoulders, Core
fields.description: Olympic weightlifting movement. Pull the bar from the floor explosively, aggressively shrug and pull with the arms as the bar reaches hip height, then drop under the bar and catch it in the front rack position. Requires significant technique practice. Develops explosive hip extension power that transfers to almost every athletic movement. Start with the hang power clean to learn the second pull before pulling from the floor.

fields.name: Dumbbell Snatch
fields.muscle_group: Full Body: Hips, Shoulders, Core, Legs
fields.description: Single-arm version of the Olympic snatch. Pull a dumbbell from between the feet explosively in one continuous motion to lockout overhead, catching it with a straight arm. The movement is all hips — not an arm pull. Requires hip flexibility, shoulder stability, and explosive power. More accessible than the barbell snatch as it requires less wrist and shoulder mobility. Develops tremendous power and athleticism.

fields.name: Medicine Ball Slam
fields.description: Raise a medicine ball overhead with full extension, then slam it into the floor as hard as possible. The slam engages the entire anterior chain (core, hip flexors, shoulders) and provides a significant stress outlet. Pick the ball up and repeat rhythmically. Develops power in the downward movement pattern and is exceptionally good for stress relief. Use a non-bouncing slam ball for safety.

fields.name: Tire Flip
fields.muscle_group: Full Body: Hamstrings, Glutes, Lower Back, Shoulders, Chest
fields.description: Approach the tire in a sumo deadlift stance, grip under the tire with palms facing up, and drive with the legs to tip the tire upward. Once it reaches a tipping point, shift hands to push position and drive it all the way over. The key is leg drive in the initial phase — this is not a back exercise. Extremely demanding for the posterior chain and cardiovascular system. A primal, brutally effective conditioning movement.

fields.name: Downward Facing Dog
fields.description: Foundational yoga pose. Start in a plank, push hips up and back to form an inverted V shape. Press through the palms, lengthen through the spine, and work toward pressing the heels toward the floor. Hold for 5 breaths, pedaling the feet to warm up the calves and hamstrings. This pose serves as both a rest position and an active stretch. Excellent for releasing tightness in the posterior chain and decompressing the spine.

fields.name: Warrior I (Virabhadrasana I)
fields.description: From a standing split stance, bend the front knee to 90 degrees (knee over ankle), back foot at 45-degree angle, hips squared forward. Raise arms overhead, palms together or parallel. Lengthen the spine upward and sink the hips downward. Hold for 30-60 seconds each side. Opens the hip flexors of the back leg and develops lower body strength and stability. Breathe deeply and maintain engagement throughout.

fields.name: Warrior II (Virabhadrasana II)
fields.description: Wide-stance lunge with arms extended horizontally in line with the legs. Front knee bent over ankle, back leg straight and strong. Hips open to the side (unlike Warrior I). Gaze over the front hand. Hold for 30-60 seconds each side. Develops hip strength, stability, and endurance. One of the most powerful grounding poses in yoga. The front thigh should ideally be parallel to the floor for maximum quad challenge.

fields.name: Pigeon Pose
fields.muscle_group: Hip Flexors, Piriformis, Glutes, IT Band
fields.description: Deep hip opener targeting the piriformis and external hip rotators. Front shin is on the floor at an angle, back leg extends behind. Fold forward over the front leg to deepen the stretch. Hold for 90 seconds to 3 minutes per side for meaningful tissue change. Excellent for people who sit for long periods and for athletes with tight hips. Modifications: use a block under the hip if the hip doesn't reach the floor.

fields.name: Child's Pose (Balasana)
fields.description: Resting pose in yoga. From kneeling, fold forward with arms extended or alongside the body. Forehead rests on the floor. Hips sink back toward heels. A deeply restorative position that gently stretches the lower back, hips, and ankles while calming the nervous system. Used as a transition and rest pose throughout yoga practice. Can remain here for several minutes as a stress-relief practice.

fields.name: Cobra Pose (Bhujangasana)
fields.description: Lie face down, hands under shoulders, elbows close to the body. Press through the palms to lift the chest off the floor, using the back muscles (not just the arms). Keep the pubic bone on the floor. Hold for 5-10 breaths. Excellent for developing spinal extension strength and counteracting the effects of prolonged sitting. A gentler alternative to upward facing dog. A daily practice can significantly reduce lower back discomfort.

fields.name: Bridge Pose (Setu Bandha Sarvangasana)
fields.description: Yoga-based glute bridge. Lie on back, feet hip-width apart and close to the glutes. Press feet into the floor, peel the spine off the mat starting from the tailbone, and rise to a full hip extension. Interlace fingers under the back for a deeper chest opener. Hold for 30-60 seconds. More meditative and breath-focused than the gym hip thrust. Excellent for spinal mobility and glute activation with a mindful approach.

fields.name: Tree Pose (Vrksasana)
fields.description: Single-leg balance pose. Stand on one foot, place the sole of the other foot against the inner thigh or calf (never the knee). Bring hands to heart center or raise overhead. Find a fixed gaze point (drishti) to help balance. Hold for 30-60 seconds each side. Develops single-leg stability, proprioception, and concentration. Excellent for athletic performance as balance is the foundation of all movement.

fields.name: Seated Forward Fold (Paschimottanasana)
fields.description: Sit with legs extended straight. Hinge at the hips (not the waist) to fold forward, reaching for the feet or ankles. Keep the spine long — avoid rounding the upper back. If hamstrings are very tight, slightly bend the knees. Hold for 60-90 seconds for lasting flexibility gains. One of the deepest hamstring stretches in yoga. Calms the nervous system and is an excellent end-of-workout stretch.

fields.name: Supine Spinal Twist (Supta Matsyendrasana)
fields.muscle_group: Spinal Rotators, IT Band, Glutes, Obliques
fields.description: Lie on back, draw one knee to the chest, then cross it over the body to the opposite side while extending that arm out to the side. Turn the gaze the opposite direction. Hold for 60-90 seconds each side. A deeply effective spinal decompression and rotation stretch. Excellent after heavy training or prolonged sitting. Often performed as the final pose before savasana to release residual tension in the back.

fields.name: Ab Wheel Rollout
fields.description: Kneel on the floor gripping an ab wheel. Extend forward slowly until the body is nearly horizontal, maintaining a straight body line. Pull back using the abs and lats — do not use the hip flexors to return. The anti-extension demand on the core is extreme. Progress from partial rollouts to full rollouts to standing rollouts. One of the most difficult core exercises available and one of the most effective for building true core strength.

fields.name: Hanging Leg Raise
fields.description: Hang from a pull-up bar with arms extended. Raise the legs to 90 degrees (knees bent for beginner, straight legs for advanced) using abdominal strength. Avoid swinging — initiate each rep from a dead stop. For advanced: raise legs all the way to the bar (toes-to-bar). The hanging position also develops grip strength. More effective than floor crunches for building lower abdominal definition.

fields.name: Russian Twist
fields.description: Sit on the floor with knees bent and feet elevated (or on the floor for easier version). Lean back to approximately 45 degrees. Hold a weight or medicine ball and rotate the torso side to side, touching the weight to the floor on each side. The movement is trunk rotation, not arm swinging. Develops oblique strength and rotational power. Excellent for sports that require rotation such as tennis, golf, and baseball.

fields.name: Dead Bug
fields.muscle_group: Transverse Abdominis, Rectus Abdominis, Core Stability
fields.description: Lie on back, arms extended toward ceiling, knees at 90 degrees above hips. Slowly lower the opposite arm and leg toward the floor simultaneously while pressing the lower back firmly into the floor. Return and switch sides. The key is maintaining lumbar contact with the floor throughout. A rehabilitation-derived exercise that teaches deep core activation and anti-extension stability. Excellent for lower back health.

fields.name: Pallof Press
fields.description: Anti-rotation core exercise using a cable machine or resistance band. Stand perpendicular to the anchor point, hands at chest. Press straight out in front of you and hold for 2-3 seconds before returning. The resistance tries to rotate you toward the anchor — your job is to resist that rotation. Excellent for developing functional core stability and injury prevention. Progress by standing farther from the anchor or using more resistance.

fields.goal: Build lean muscle mass and increase overall strength. Currently at 82 kg with a goal of reaching 90 kg at around 12% body fat. Been lifting casually for 2 years but never followed a structured program. Want to learn proper technique on all major compound lifts and build a solid foundation before moving to more advanced programming.

Lose 10 kg over the next 6 months through a combination of strength training and cardio. Desk job means very little daily activity. Main motivation is an upcoming wedding (both attending and wanting to look and feel great). Interested in toning arms and reducing belly fat specifically.

General health improvement after doctor's recommendation. Borderline high blood pressure and cholesterol. Wants to lose 12-15 kg and improve cardiovascular health. Has not exercised regularly in 10 years. Needs a gradual introduction to exercise with close monitoring of intensity. Also interested in stress management.

Preparing for a half-marathon in 4 months. Current best 10K time is 54 minutes, aiming for sub-50. Also wants to incorporate strength training to improve running economy and prevent injuries. Specifically needs to strengthen the glutes and hips which are identified as weak points.

Achieve a competitive level physique for a men's physique competition in 7 months. Currently approximately 15% body fat, targeting 6-7%. Experienced lifter with 5 years of training but first time working with a professional coach. Wants expert guidance on periodization, nutrition timing, and peak week protocol.

Tone up and gain confidence before returning to the dating scene after a long relationship. Wants to lose approximately 5 kg, improve posture, and feel stronger and more energetic. Particularly interested in working on the lower body and core. Prefers group classes over one-on-one training for the social element.

Significant weight loss (25 kg target) and lifestyle transformation. Type 2 diabetic — exercise has been prescribed by endocrinologist. Needs low-impact options initially due to knee osteoarthritis. Committed to making permanent changes and understands this is a long-term journey. Has completely revamped diet simultaneously.

Improve flexibility, core strength and manage chronic lower back pain that has been an issue for 3 years. Works as a nurse with long standing shifts. Interested in yoga and Pilates-based approaches alongside targeted strength work. Goal is to be pain-free and mobile for the long term, not primarily aesthetic.

Build a strong athletic physique and improve performance in recreational football. Plays in a Sunday league and wants to be faster, stronger, and have better endurance. Currently skinny-fat — wants to gain 7-8 kg of muscle while losing a little fat. Training 4 times per week.

Run a full marathon next year. This year completed first half-marathon in 2:15. Building a serious endurance base with a long-term plan. Also wants to incorporate cross-training (strength and mobility work) to support running and stay injury-free. Particularly prone to IT band tightness.

Maintain functional strength and muscle mass as an active 50-year-old. Recent health check showed early signs of sarcopenia. Doctor recommended resistance training at least 3x per week. Also wants to maintain the ability to play golf twice a week without pain or fatigue. Interested in longevity-focused training.

Transition from years of long-distance running to strength training. Has good cardiovascular base but very little upper body strength. Wants to build a balanced physique and break out of the 'runner's body' look. Goals include overhead pressing her bodyweight and squatting 1.5x bodyweight within 12 months.

Improve sports performance for amateur cycling and weekend skiing. Wants to develop leg power, core stability, and upper body endurance specific to cycling. Racing first gran fondo in 6 months. Needs structured periodization that allows for 10 hours of cycling per week plus gym work.

Compete in Olympic-style weightlifting within 18 months. Currently completing beginner barbell work. Attracted by the technical nature of the sport after watching the Olympics. Needs to develop mobility, technique, and strength simultaneously. Willing to commit fully — 5 sessions per week.

Lose weight and build a healthier relationship with exercise. Previous attempts at diet and exercise have always resulted in burnout and weight regain. Looking for a sustainable approach this time. Wants to understand the principles behind training and nutrition rather than just following a plan blindly. Enjoys outdoor activities when fit.

Deepen yoga practice and develop the strength to perform advanced poses including handstand, forearm stand, and full splits. Currently practices yoga 3x per week at intermediate level. Wants to add strength training to support the practice and address weakness in shoulders and core that are limiting progress.

Classic physique bodybuilding competition in 10 months. Has trained for 4 years and has good muscle development but lacks conditioning knowledge. Wants guidance on training splits, contest prep nutrition, and posing. Previous competition experience is zero — first time competitor seeking professional coaching.

Post-pregnancy fitness restoration 14 months after C-section. Core has been significantly weakened and there is a small diastasis recti. Cleared by physiotherapist to begin exercise. Needs specialist programming that avoids exercises that worsen the abdominal separation while rebuilding core strength and overall fitness.

CEO with extremely limited time — maximum 3x 45-minute sessions per week. Wants the most efficient path to losing 15 kg and improving energy levels. Suffers from chronic fatigue and low testosterone (under medical supervision). Training must be time-efficient and evidence-based. Results-oriented mindset.

Gain strength and weight in a healthy way. Currently underweight due to a history of disordered eating (fully recovered, under ongoing clinical supervision). Working with a registered dietitian simultaneously. Wants to build a positive relationship with exercise focused on capability rather than appearance. Seeking a supportive, non-aesthetic-focused approach.

Compete in first powerlifting meet in the 83 kg class within 8 months. Currently training with estimated maxes of: squat 120 kg, bench 90 kg, deadlift 140 kg. Wants to compete with numbers of 140/100/170. Needs competition-specific programming, meet selection advice, and technical coaching on all three lifts.

ACL rehabilitation — 6 months post-surgery from a skiing accident. Physio has signed off on gym-based training. Wants to return to full skiing ability within 12 months. Needs progressive loading of the operated knee, quad strengthening, proprioception training, and eventually plyometric work for return to sport.

Become a competitive CrossFit athlete at the regional level. Currently competing at local box level with solid gymnastics but weak Olympic lifting. Needs to significantly improve snatch and clean & jerk while maintaining gymnastics skills and engine. Willing to train 2x per day 5 days per week.

Triathlon preparation for a sprint triathlon in 5 months and an Olympic distance event in 9 months. Currently a decent runner but weak swimmer and never done structured cycling training. Needs a comprehensive cross-training plan that develops all three disciplines without overtraining.

Lose the 'dad bod' gained over the past 5 years of parenting and work stress. Used to be athletic in his 20s (played university rugby) and wants to recapture that fitness level. Family man so workouts must be early morning or lunchtime. Realistic about the timeline — targeting 18 months.

Managing perimenopause symptoms through exercise. Recent research shows heavy resistance training significantly helps with menopausal symptoms — interested in implementing this. Also wants to protect bone density. Has never done serious weight training but is very motivated and health-literate.

Prepare for an Ironman triathlon in 14 months. Experienced cyclist but new to structured run and swim training. Currently can run 10K in 55 minutes and swim 400m comfortably. Training plan must integrate with approximately 12-15 hours of sport-specific training per week. Gym work must be complementary.

Bikini fitness competition preparation. Has competed twice before at local level and wants to step up to national competitions. Needs advanced programming with detailed periodization, posing coaching, and peak week guidance. Very experienced with dieting and training — needs a coach who understands competition prep at a high level.

Exercise prescription for Type 2 diabetes management — exercise is part of a medical management plan alongside medication. Doctor has requested 150+ minutes of moderate aerobic activity per week plus 2 strength sessions. Needs careful monitoring and initially very conservative progression. Long-term goal is to reduce medication dependency.

Improve athleticism for semi-professional dance career. Needs explosive power, flexibility, and endurance specific to contemporary dance. Recently joined a professional company and competition for roles is fierce. Wants to be the most physically prepared dancer in the company. Also needs injury prevention work for ankles and hips.

Qualify for the CrossFit Open top 25% in his age category next year. Currently in the top 40%. Needs to improve gymnastics skills (particularly handstand push-ups and toes-to-bar), Olympic lifting percentages, and overall engine. Has a solid strength base from 3 years of regular training.

Re-enter the fitness world after 8 years away due to career demands and family commitments. Was previously very fit and sporty in her 30s. Wants a complete reset and rebuild. Open to any type of training that works. Main goals: feel energetic, look good, and be strong enough to enjoy hiking holidays.

Develop strength and speed for university-level football (soccer). Plays as a central midfielder and needs to develop explosive first-step speed, jumping ability, and ability to hold off opponents physically. Currently too light and weak for the physicality of university play. Target: gain 5-6 kg of muscle before next season.

Postpartum fitness 8 months after birth of second child. Suffered significant diastasis recti with first pregnancy (fully resolved). Wants to prevent recurrence with correct programming. Also has weak pelvic floor. Working alongside a women's health physiotherapist. Goals are strength and fitness with special attention to core rehabilitation.

Gain 10 kg of quality muscle mass over 18 months. Hardgainer who has struggled to gain weight despite eating well. Previous training has been unfocused. Wants a proper hypertrophy program with exercise selection, progressive overload planning, and guidance on caloric surplus nutrition. Has good recovery capacity and can train 5 days per week.

Use yoga and mindful movement as a tool for mental health management alongside therapy. Has anxiety disorder and has found that structured breathing and movement practice significantly helps symptom management. Wants to develop a consistent daily practice and eventually train to teach yoga to others. Certification in mind.

Complete body transformation — lose 20 kg and completely change lifestyle. Quit smoking 6 months ago and wants to channel that discipline into fitness. Works from home which means easy access to food and sedentary habits. Needs accountability coaching and lifestyle change support alongside the physical training program.

Prepare for a fitness model photoshoot in 16 weeks. Already lean and fit from years of gymnastics background. Needs to develop specific areas: rounder glutes, fuller shoulders, and tighter midsection. Experienced enough to train intensely. Needs expert programming for the final push to competition shape.

Eliminate chronic lower back pain that has been affecting quality of life for 5 years. MRI shows disc bulge at L4-L5 with no surgical recommendation. Physio has recommended core strengthening and hip mobility work. Also wants to lose 8 kg. Has been scared to exercise but physiotherapist says movement is medicine.

Achieve full front splits and middle splits as a long-term flexibility goal, inspired by watching gymnastics. Currently has moderate flexibility from years of dance. Interested in combining yoga, assisted stretching, and strength work to improve. Also wants to improve general fitness and energy levels.

Train for the World's Toughest Mudder — a 24-hour obstacle course race. Needs extraordinary endurance, strength, and mental toughness. 10 months to prepare. Has completed several shorter OCRs including a Tough Mudder. Needs to dramatically increase running endurance, grip strength, and ability to perform under fatigue.

Run the London Marathon next April. First marathon attempt. Current weekly mileage is 30 km. Needs to build to 60+ km peak weeks while staying injury-free. Interested in adding strength training to prevent the common running injuries she's experienced in the past (shin splints, ITBS). Wants a holistic approach to marathon training.

Heart attack survivor 18 months ago — cleared for progressive exercise by cardiologist. Started a cardiac rehab program and has completed it. Now ready for more challenging supervised exercise. Goal is to lose weight, strengthen the heart, and eventually return to gentle hiking. Every session needs heart rate monitoring.

Build a strong, curvy physique with emphasis on glutes and hamstrings. Inspired by powerlifting athletes and the strong is beautiful movement. Has been training casually for 1 year with mostly cardio. Ready to commit to serious strength training and build the foundation for a powerful physique.

Get in peak physical condition for military officer selection in 9 months. Selection tests include: 1.5 mile run in under 10:30, 44 push-ups in 2 minutes, 50 sit-ups in 2 minutes, and a swimming test. Currently meets some standards but wants significant headroom. Highly motivated with strong discipline.

Manage menopause symptoms naturally through exercise and lifestyle. Experiencing hot flashes, sleep disruption, and mood changes. Recent bone density scan showed early osteopenia. Endocrinologist recommended weight-bearing exercise and resistance training. Wants to feel empowered by her body during this life transition.

Pursue powerlifting at the masters level (40+ category, 93 kg class). Currently 37 and wanting to establish a strong competition record before reaching masters age. Current totals: squat 160, bench 115, deadlift 195. Aiming for elite total in the 93 kg class within 3 years.

Transform from slim but untoned to lean and athletic. Currently very lightly active. Works in fashion and wants to look fit and healthy rather than just thin. Wants functional strength, improved posture, toned arms and legs. Interested in a balanced program mixing strength, cardio, and mobility work.

Develop serious endurance fitness from a complete beginner base. Never exercised consistently in life despite always wanting to. Wants to run a 10K within 6 months, then build from there. Long-term dream is an ultramarathon in 3-4 years. Needs structured progressive build and help establishing the habit of training.

Prevent osteoporosis and maintain bone density through weight-bearing exercise. Family history of osteoporosis — mother suffered a hip fracture at 65. DEXA scan shows osteopenia. Rheumatologist has strongly recommended resistance training. Also wants to improve balance and reduce fall risk as part of healthy aging strategy.

fields.name: Starting Strength 5×5
fields.description: Classic linear progression program built around the three major barbell lifts performed three times per week. Each session alternates between Workout A (squat, bench, deadlift) and Workout B (squat, overhead press, deadlift or power clean). Add weight every session — 2.5 kg on upper body lifts, 5 kg on squats and deadlift. Designed to build strength and muscle mass simultaneously in beginners and intermediate lifters. Rest 3-5 minutes between heavy work sets. Track every session meticulously.

fields.name: HIIT Metabolic Blast
fields.description: High-intensity interval training protocol designed for maximum caloric expenditure and cardiovascular adaptation in minimum time. 40 minutes total: 7-minute warm-up, 25 minutes of work (8 exercises, 3 rounds, 40 sec on / 20 sec off), 8-minute cool-down. Heart rate should reach 85-95% of maximum during work periods. Suitable for intermediate and advanced fitness levels. Expect to burn 400-600 calories per session.

fields.name: Morning Yoga Flow
fields.description: 60-minute energizing morning yoga practice designed to wake up the body and prepare the mind for the day ahead. Begins with 5 minutes of pranayama (box breathing), flows through a dynamic sun salutation sequence, progresses through standing balance poses, and finishes with 10 minutes of deep stretching and savasana. All levels welcome. Props (blocks, straps) available for modifications. Regular practice improves flexibility, mental clarity, posture, and stress resilience.

fields.name: Fat Burning Cardio Circuit
fields.description: 45-minute cardio program combining steady-state aerobic work with higher-intensity intervals to maximize fat oxidation. Structure: 8-minute warm-up on treadmill, 30 minutes alternating between 3-minute moderate steady-state and 1-minute intervals, 7-minute cool-down and stretch. Heart rate zones: 65-70% for steady-state, 80-85% for intervals. Highly suitable for beginners to intermediate. Progresses over 8 weeks from 3 to 5 sessions per week.

fields.name: Functional Total Body
fields.description: 60-minute full-body functional training session emphasizing movements that transfer directly to athletic performance and daily life. Uses kettlebells, bodyweight, and resistance bands. 5 blocks of 4 exercises performed as supersets. Develops strength, coordination, power, and endurance simultaneously. Suitable for intermediate to advanced athletes. No machine work — all functional movement patterns: hinge, squat, push, pull, carry, rotate.

fields.name: Powerlifting Foundations
fields.description: 12-week introductory powerlifting program teaching the competition squat, bench press, and deadlift with competition legal technique. Includes daily maxes, RPE-based training, and competition simulation. Heavy emphasis on technique before loading. Accessories selected to address common weaknesses in each lift. Sessions 90-120 minutes. Designed to prepare novice powerlifters for their first competition or serious strength athletes wanting a structured base.

fields.name: Beginner Full Body Strength
fields.description: Perfect entry point for those new to resistance training. Three full-body sessions per week covering all major movement patterns: squat, hinge, push, pull, and carry. Emphasis on learning correct technique with manageable loads before adding progressive overload. Sessions 45-50 minutes including warm-up and cool-down. Suitable for complete beginners and those returning after a long break. Progress is tracked and load increases every 1-2 weeks.

fields.name: Advanced Hypertrophy (PPL)
fields.description: Push-Pull-Legs split designed for intermediate to advanced lifters focused on maximizing muscle growth. Six sessions per week (Push/Pull/Legs x2). High volume with controlled intensity — 4 sets of 8-12 reps per exercise, 6-8 exercises per session. Includes both compound and isolation movements with emphasis on mind-muscle connection, progressive overload, and adequate recovery. Nutrition must support muscle growth for best results.

fields.name: Athletic Performance Training
fields.description: Sport-specific performance program for competitive and recreational athletes. Combines explosive power development (Olympic lifting derivatives, plyometrics), strength training in relevant movement patterns, and energy system work. Periodized over 16 weeks with phases focusing on: general preparation, strength, power, and competition peak. Suitable for athletes competing in team sports, martial arts, combat sports, and track and field.

fields.name: Senior Mobility & Strength
fields.description: Gentle but effective 45-minute program designed for adults over 50 prioritizing functional independence, bone density, and quality of life. Combines resistance training with mobility work, balance training, and flexibility. All exercises adaptable to seated versions if needed. Focus on movement quality, not intensity. Sessions 3x per week. Proven to improve grip strength, balance, bone density, and cognitive function. Social atmosphere encouraged.

fields.name: Upper-Lower Power Split
fields.description: 4-day upper-lower training split emphasizing power development and hypertrophy. Upper days include heavy pressing and pulling; lower days focus on squatting and hinging patterns. Each session starts with a power focus (heavier, lower rep, explosive) then transitions to hypertrophy work. Ideal for intermediate lifters wanting to build strength and size concurrently. Recovery is built in with only 4 sessions per week.

fields.name: Kettlebell Strength Circuit
fields.description: 45-minute kettlebell-focused program that develops total body strength, conditioning, and movement quality. Built around the six fundamental kettlebell movements: swing, goblet squat, clean, press, snatch, and Turkish get-up. Three circuits with targeted rest periods. Suitable for those with access to only kettlebells or preferring minimal equipment training. Develops functional strength that transfers directly to daily life and athletic performance.

fields.name: Core & Stability Foundations
fields.description: Targeted 40-minute core strengthening program addressing deep stability, anti-movement strength, and rotational power. Goes beyond sit-ups to address all functions of the core: anti-flexion, anti-extension, anti-rotation, and lateral stability. Suitable for all levels with clear progressions and regressions for each exercise. Highly recommended as a supplementary program for lifters, runners, and those with history of back pain.

fields.name: Flexibility & Active Recovery
fields.description: 60-minute restorative session combining active stretching, yoga-based mobility work, and foam rolling. Designed to accelerate recovery between harder training sessions, improve long-term flexibility, and address postural imbalances. Suitable for any fitness level and particularly valuable for those with desk-based lifestyles. Can be scheduled on rest days from intense training. Includes guided breathing work to activate the parasympathetic nervous system.

fields.name: Sprint & Endurance Development
fields.description: Progressive 8-week cardio program systematically developing both aerobic base and anaerobic speed. Week 1-3: aerobic base building with longer, moderate efforts. Week 4-6: introduction of tempo runs and lactate threshold work. Week 7-8: speed work and race-pace intervals. Suitable for those preparing for running events from 5K to half-marathon. Includes weekly long run, tempo run, interval session, and easy recovery run.

fields.name: Boxing Conditioning
fields.description: Combat sports-inspired conditioning program that develops cardiovascular fitness, coordination, and upper body endurance using boxing techniques and corresponding conditioning work. No sparring — shadow boxing, bag work, and conditioning drills only. 45-minute sessions including skipping warm-up, technique work, bag rounds, and floor conditioning. Excellent for those who find traditional cardio boring. Burns 400-700 calories per session.

fields.name: Body Recomposition 12-Week
fields.description: Simultaneous fat loss and muscle gain program for intermediate trainees. Achieves body recomposition through a combination of strength training stimulus (to preserve and build muscle) and moderate cardio (to create caloric deficit). Caloric intake is cycled — higher on training days, lower on rest days. 4 strength sessions and 2 cardio sessions per week. Requires disciplined nutrition tracking for best results.

fields.name: Post-Rehabilitation Strength
fields.description: Carefully designed strength program for individuals returning from injury under medical clearance. Progressive loading from very light to moderate over 12 weeks. All exercises selected to avoid loading the injured area while maintaining overall fitness. Emphasis on movement quality, proprioception, and addressing muscular imbalances that may have caused the injury. Regular reassessment every 3 weeks. Close communication with physiotherapist required.

fields.name: Competition Peak & Taper
fields.description: 4-week peaking program designed to maximize performance for an upcoming powerlifting or strength competition. Volume decreases progressively while intensity peaks. Includes competition-style practice attempts, opener selection work, and a 1-week deload taper before competition. Addresses competition day logistics: warm-up protocol, attempt selection strategy, and equipment handling. Designed for experienced competitors, not beginners.

fields.name: Women's Strength & Sculpt
fields.description: Comprehensive 16-week program designed specifically for women wanting to build strength and develop an athletic physique. Prioritizes posterior chain development (glutes, hamstrings, back), overhead pressing strength, and hip hinge mechanics. Systematically progressive over four 4-week blocks. Includes dedicated glute work in every session. Heavy enough to drive real strength gains — no pink dumbbells. Full guide on nutrition for body recomposition included.
